# Single-cell transcriptomics

**Single-cell transcriptomics** examines the [gene](/wiki/Gene "Gene") expression level of individual [cells](/wiki/Cell_(biology) "Cell (biology)") in a given population by simultaneously measuring the RNA concentration, typically [messenger RNA](/wiki/Messenger_RNA "Messenger RNA") (mRNA), of hundreds to thousands of [genes](/wiki/Gene "Gene").[[1]](#cite_note-Kanter_2015_53-1) Single-cell transcriptomics makes it possible to unravel [heterogeneous](/wiki/Heterogenous "Heterogenous") cell populations, reconstruct [cellular developmental](/wiki/Cell_development "Cell development") pathways, and model transcriptional dynamics—all previously masked in bulk RNA sequencing.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Background

The development of high-throughput [RNA sequencing](/wiki/RNA_sequencing "RNA sequencing") (RNA-seq) and [microarrays](/wiki/Microarrays "Microarrays") has made gene expression analysis a routine. RNA analysis was previously limited to tracing individual [transcripts](/wiki/Transcription_(biology) "Transcription (biology)") by [Northern blots](/wiki/Northern_blot "Northern blot") or [quantitative PCR](/wiki/Quantitative_PCR "Quantitative PCR"). Higher throughput and speed allow researchers to frequently characterize the expression profiles of populations of thousands of cells. The data from bulk [assays](/wiki/Assays "Assays") has led to identifying genes differentially expressed in distinct cell populations, and [biomarker](/wiki/Biomarker "Biomarker") discovery.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/RNA_Seq_Experiment.png/330px-RNA_Seq_Experiment.png)

These studies are limited as they provide measurements for whole tissues and, as a result, show an average expression profile for all the constituent cells. This has a couple of drawbacks. Firstly, different cell types within the same tissue can have distinct roles in multicellular organisms. They often form subpopulations with unique transcriptional profiles. Correlations in the gene expression of the subpopulations can often be missed due to the lack of subpopulation identification.[[1]](#cite_note-Kanter_2015_53-1) Secondly, bulk assays fail to recognize whether a change in the expression profile is due to a change in regulation or composition — for example if one cell type arises to dominate the population. Lastly, when your goal is to study cellular progression through [differentiation](/wiki/Cellular_differentiation "Cellular differentiation"), average expression profiles can only order cells by time rather than by developmental stage. Consequently, they cannot show trends in gene expression levels specific to certain stages.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Recent advances in biotechnology allow the measurement of gene expression in hundreds to thousands of individual cells simultaneously. While these breakthroughs in [transcriptomics technologies](/wiki/Transcriptomics_technologies "Transcriptomics technologies") have enabled the generation of single-cell transcriptomic data, they also presented new computational and analytical challenges. Bioinformaticians can use techniques from bulk RNA-seq for single-cell data. Still, many new computational approaches have had to be designed for this data type to facilitate a complete and detailed study of single-cell expression profiles.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Experimental steps

There is so far no standardized technique to generate single-cell data: all methods must include cell isolation from the population, [lysate](/wiki/Lysate "Lysate") formation, amplification through [reverse transcription](/wiki/Reverse_transcription "Reverse transcription"), and quantification of expression levels. Common techniques for measuring expression are quantitative PCR or RNA-seq.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

### Isolating single cells

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Fluorescence_Assisted_Cell_Sorting_%28FACS%29_B2.jpg/250px-Fluorescence_Assisted_Cell_Sorting_%28FACS%29_B2.jpg)

Several methods are available to isolate and amplify cells for single-cell analysis, differing primarily in throughput and potential for cell selection. Low-throughput techniques, such as [micropipetting](/w/index.php?title=Micropipetting&action=edit&redlink=1 "Micropipetting (page does not exist)"), cytoplasmic aspiration,[[7]](#cite_note-7) and [laser capture microdissection](/wiki/Laser_capture_microdissection "Laser capture microdissection"), typically isolate hundreds of cells but enable deliberate cell selection.

High-throughput methods allow for the rapid isolation of hundreds to tens of thousands of cells.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Common high-throughput approaches include [Fluorescence Activated Cell Sorting](/wiki/Fluorescence_activated_cell_sorting "Fluorescence activated cell sorting") (FACS) and the use of [microfluidic devices](/wiki/Microfluidic_device "Microfluidic device"). Microfluidic platforms often isolate single cells either by mechanical separation into microwells (e.g., BD Rhapsody, Takara ICELL8, Vycap Puncher Platform, CellMicrosystems CellRaft) or by encapsulation within droplets (e.g., 10x Genomics Chromium, Illumina Bio-Rad ddSEQ, 1CellBio InDrop, Dolomite Bio Nadia).[[9]](#cite_note-9) Furthermore, optimized protocols have been developed by integrating these isolation techniques directly with scRNA-seq workflows. For instance, combining FACS with scRNA-seq led to protocols like SORT-seq,[[10]](#cite_note-10) and a list of studies utilizing SORT-seq can be found here.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Similarly, the integration of microfluidic devices with scRNA-seq has been highly optimized in protocols such as those developed by [10x Genomics](/wiki/10x_Genomics "10x Genomics").[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Single cell RNA-seq techniques that rely on split-pool barcoding can uniquely label cells without requiring the isolation of individual cells, including sci-RNA-seq, SPLiT-seq, and microSPLiT.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### Quantitative PCR (qPCR)

To measure the level of expression of each transcript qPCR can be applied. Gene specific [primers](/wiki/Primer_(molecular_biology) "Primer (molecular biology)") are used to amplify the corresponding gene as with regular [PCR](/wiki/Polymerase_chain_reaction "Polymerase chain reaction") and as a result data is usually only obtained for sample sizes of less than 100 genes. The inclusion of [housekeeping genes](/wiki/Housekeeping_genes "Housekeeping genes"), whose expression should be constant under the conditions, is used for normalization. The most commonly used house keeping genes include [GAPDH](/wiki/GAPDH "GAPDH") and α-[actin](/wiki/Actin "Actin"), although the reliability of normalization through this process is questionable as there is evidence that the level of expression can vary significantly.[[16]](#cite_note-16) [Fluorescent dyes](/wiki/Fluorescent_dyes "Fluorescent dyes") are used as [reporter molecules](/wiki/Reporter_gene "Reporter gene") to detect the PCR product and monitor the progress of the amplification - the increase in fluorescence intensity is proportional to the [amplicon](/wiki/Amplicon "Amplicon") concentration. A plot of fluorescence vs. cycle number is made and a threshold fluorescence level is used to find cycle number at which the plot reaches this value. The cycle number at this point is known as the threshold cycle (Ct) and is measured for each gene.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### Single-cell RNA-seq (scRNA-Seq)

The [single-cell](/wiki/Single-cell_sequencing "Single-cell sequencing") [RNA-seq](/wiki/RNA-Seq "RNA-Seq") technique converts a population of RNAs to a library of [cDNA](/wiki/CDNA "CDNA") fragments that can be sequenced. In droplet-based technologies such as 10x Genomics Chromium, single cells are isolated in droplets together with beads coated with barcoded oligonucleotides. Both cells and beads are supplied in limited amounts such that co-occupancy with multiple cells and beads is a very rare event. Cells are lysed within the droplets, and RNAs are reverse transcribed using the barcoded oligo-dT oligonucleotides as primers. After reverse transcription, the emulsion is broken, releasing the barcoded cDNA from all the droplets into a single solution. This pooled cDNA is then prepared for sequencing via the addition of sequencing adapters and PCR amplification.

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/RNA-Seq_workflow-5.pdf/page1-280px-RNA-Seq_workflow-5.pdf.jpg)

These fragments are sequenced by high-throughput [next generation sequencing](/wiki/Next_generation_sequencing "Next generation sequencing") techniques and the reads are mapped back to the reference genome, providing a count of the number of reads associated with each gene.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Transcripts from a particular cell are identified by each cell's unique barcode.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Normalization of RNA-Seq data accounts for cell to cell variation in the efficiencies of the cDNA library formation and sequencing. One method relies on the use of [extrinsic](/wiki/Extrinsic "Extrinsic") [RNA spike-ins](/wiki/RNA_spike-in "RNA spike-in") that are added in equal quantities to each cell [lysate](/wiki/Lysate "Lysate") and used to normalize read count by the number of reads mapped to spike-in [mRNA](/wiki/Messenger_RNA "Messenger RNA").[[21]](#cite_note-21) Another control uses [unique molecular identifiers](/wiki/Unique_molecular_identifiers "Unique molecular identifiers") (UMIs)-short DNA sequences (6–10nt) that are added to each cDNA before amplification and act as a bar code for each cDNA molecule. Normalization is achieved by using the count number of unique UMIs associated with each gene to account for differences in amplification efficiency.[[22]](#cite_note-Quantitative_single-cell_RNA-seq_wi-22)

A combination of both [spike-ins](/wiki/Spike-ins "Spike-ins"), UMIs and other approaches have been combined to help identify artifacts during library preparation[[23]](#cite_note-23) and for more accurate normalization.

#### Applications

scRNA-Seq is becoming widely used across biological disciplines including Development, [Neurology](/wiki/Neurology "Neurology"),[[24]](#cite_note-24) [Oncology](/wiki/Oncology "Oncology"),[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27) [Autoimmune disease](/wiki/Autoimmune_disease "Autoimmune disease"),[[28]](#cite_note-28), [Infectious disease](/wiki/Infectious_disease_(medical_specialty) "Infectious disease (medical specialty)").[[29]](#cite_note-29), [brain disease](/wiki/Brain_disease "Brain disease"),[[30]](#cite_note-30) and environmental virology [[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32). Several scRNA-Seq protocols have been published: Tang et al.,[[33]](#cite_note-33) STRT,[[34]](#cite_note-34) SMART-seq,[[35]](#cite_note-35) CEL-seq,[[36]](#cite_note-36) RAGE-seq,[[37]](#cite_note-37) Quartz-seq[[38]](#cite_note-38) and C1-CAGE.[[39]](#cite_note-39) These protocols differ in terms of strategies for reverse transcription, cDNA synthesis and amplification, and the possibility to accommodate sequence-specific barcodes (i.e. [UMIs](/wiki/Unique_molecular_identifier "Unique molecular identifier")) or the ability to process pooled samples.[[40]](#cite_note-40) In 2017, two approaches were introduced to simultaneously measure single-cell mRNA and protein expression through oligonucleotide-labeled antibodies known as REAP-seq,[[41]](#cite_note-41) and CITE-seq.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

scRNA-Seq has provided considerable insight into the development of embryos and organisms, including the worm *[Caenorhabditis elegans](/wiki/Caenorhabditis_elegans "Caenorhabditis elegans")*,[[43]](#cite_note-43) and the regenerative planarian *[Schmidtea mediterranea](/wiki/Schmidtea_mediterranea "Schmidtea mediterranea")*.[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45) The first vertebrate animals to be mapped in this way were [Zebrafish](/wiki/Zebrafish "Zebrafish")[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47) and *[Xenopus laevis](/wiki/Xenopus_laevis "Xenopus laevis")*.[[48]](#cite_note-48) In each case multiple stages of the embryo were studied, allowing the entire process of development to be mapped on a cell-by-cell basis.[[49]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-49) [Science](/wiki/Science_Magazine "Science Magazine") recognized these advances as the 2018 [Breakthrough of the Year](/wiki/Breakthrough_of_the_Year "Breakthrough of the Year").[[50]](#cite_note-50)

### Considerations

A problem associated with single-cell data occurs in the form of zero inflated gene expression distributions, known as technical dropouts, that are common due to low mRNA concentrations of less-expressed genes that are not captured in the reverse transcription process. The percentage of mRNA molecules in the cell lysate that are detected is often only 10-20%.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

When using RNA spike-ins for normalization the assumption is made that the amplification and sequencing efficiencies for the [endogenous](/wiki/Endogenous "Endogenous") and spike-in RNA are the same. Evidence suggests that this is not the case given fundamental differences in size and features, such as the lack of a [polyadenylated](/wiki/Polyadenylation "Polyadenylation") tail in spike-ins and therefore shorter length.[[52]](#cite_note-52) Additionally, normalization using UMIs assumes the cDNA library is sequenced to saturation, which is not always the case.[[22]](#cite_note-Quantitative_single-cell_RNA-seq_wi-22)

In the amplification step, either PCR or [in vitro](/wiki/In_vitro "In vitro") transcription (IVT) is currently used to amplify cDNA. One of the advantages of PCR-based methods is the ability to generate full-length cDNA. However, different PCR efficiency on particular sequences (for instance, GC content and snapback structure) may also be exponentially amplified, producing libraries with uneven coverage. On the other hand, while libraries generated by IVT can avoid PCR-induced sequence bias, specific sequences may be transcribed inefficiently, thus causing sequence drop-out or generating incomplete sequences.[[53]](#cite_note-Eberwine-53)[[54]](#cite_note-Shapiro-54)

Challenges for scRNA-Seq include preserving the initial relative abundance of mRNA in a cell and identifying rare transcripts.[[55]](#cite_note-Hebenstreit-55) The reverse transcription step is critical as the efficiency of the RT reaction determines how much of the cell's RNA population will be eventually analyzed by the sequencer. The processivity of reverse transcriptases and the priming strategies used may affect full-length cDNA production and the generation of libraries biased toward the 3’ or 5' end of genes.

A further consideration when sequencing large, branched cell types, such as [neurons](/wiki/Neurons "Neurons"), comes from the removal of distal processes containing local pools of RNA during the single-cell isolation process. In these cells, scRNA-seq datasets only capture transcript in the central cell body, omitting transcripts from RNA pools localized to cellular processes that can be involved in local translation or other RNA-mediated subcellular mechanisms. In the brain it has been estimated that over 40% of total RNA is not sequenced by scRNA-seq due to the prevalence of local transcriptomes in cellular processes such as [axons](/wiki/Axons "Axons"), [dendrites](/wiki/Dendrites "Dendrites"), [myelin](/wiki/Myelin "Myelin"), and [endfeet](/wiki/Endfeet "Endfeet").[[56]](#cite_note-56)

## Data analysis

Insights based on single-cell data analysis assume that the input is a matrix of normalized gene expression counts, generated by the approaches outlined above, and can provide opportunities that are not obtainable by bulk.

Three main insights provided:[[57]](#cite_note-Computational_and_analytical_challe-57)

The techniques outlined have been designed to help visualise and explore patterns in the data in order to facilitate the revelation of these three features.

### Clustering

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/KMeans-Gaussian-data.svg/250px-KMeans-Gaussian-data.svg.png)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Iris_dendrogram.png/250px-Iris_dendrogram.png)

[Clustering](/wiki/Subgroup_analysis "Subgroup analysis") allows for the formation of subgroups in the cell population. Cells can be clustered by their transcriptomic profile in order to analyse the sub-population structure and identify rare cell types or cell subtypes. Alternatively, genes can be clustered by their expression states in order to identify covarying genes. A combination of both clustering approaches, known as [biclustering](/wiki/Biclustering "Biclustering"), has been used to simultaneously cluster by genes and cells to find genes that behave similarly within cell clusters.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

Clustering methods applied can be [K-means clustering](/wiki/K-means_clustering "K-means clustering"), forming disjoint groups or [Hierarchical clustering](/wiki/Hierarchical_clustering "Hierarchical clustering"), forming nested partitions.

#### Biclustering

Biclustering provides several advantages by improving the resolution of clustering. Genes that are only informative to a subset of cells and are hence only expressed there can be identified through biclustering. Moreover, similarly behaving genes that differentiate one cell cluster from another can be identified using this method.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

### Dimensionality reduction

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/PCA_of_Guinean_and_other_African_populations_Y_chromosome_haplogroup_frequencies.jpg/250px-PCA_of_Guinean_and_other_African_populations_Y_chromosome_haplogroup_frequencies.jpg)

[Dimensionality reduction](/wiki/Dimensionality_reduction "Dimensionality reduction") algorithms such as [Principal component analysis](/wiki/Principal_component_analysis "Principal component analysis") (PCA) and [t-SNE](/wiki/T-SNE "T-SNE") can be used to simplify data for visualisation and pattern detection by transforming cells from a high to a lower [dimensional space](/wiki/Dimensional_space "Dimensional space"). The result of this method produces graphs with each cell as a point in a 2-D or 3-D space. Dimensionality reduction is frequently used before clustering as cells in high dimensions can wrongly appear to be close due to distance metrics behaving non-intuitively.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

#### Principal component analysis

The most frequently used technique is PCA, which identifies the directions of largest [variance](/wiki/Variance "Variance") [principal components](/wiki/Principal_components "Principal components") and transforms the data so that the first principal component has the largest possible variance, and successive principle components in turn each have the highest variance possible while remaining orthogonal to the preceding components. The contribution each gene makes to each component is used to infer which genes are contributing the most to variance in the population and are involved in differentiating different subpopulations.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

### Differential expression

Detecting differences in gene expression level between two populations is used both single-cell and bulk transcriptomic data. Specialised methods have been designed for single-cell data that considers single cell features such as technical dropouts and shape of the distribution e.g. [Bimodal](/wiki/Bimodal "Bimodal") vs. [unimodal](/wiki/Unimodal "Unimodal").[[62]](#cite_note-62)

#### Gene ontology enrichment

[Gene ontology](/wiki/Gene_ontology "Gene ontology") terms describe gene functions and the relationships between those functions into three classes:

[Gene Ontology (GO) term enrichment](/wiki/Gene_Ontology_Term_Enrichment "Gene Ontology Term Enrichment") is a technique used to identify which GO terms are over-represented or under-represented in a given set of genes. In single-cell analysis input list of genes of interest can be selected based on differentially expressed genes or groups of genes generated from biclustering. The number of genes annotated to a GO term in the input list is normalized against the number of genes annotated to a GO term in the background set of all genes in genome to determine statistical significance.[[63]](#cite_note-hag1-63)

### Pseudotemporal ordering

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/7n_graph_with_minimal_spanning_tree.svg/200px-7n_graph_with_minimal_spanning_tree.svg.png)

Pseudo-temporal ordering (or trajectory inference) is a technique that aims to infer gene expression dynamics from snapshot single-cell data. The method tries to order the cells in such a way that similar cells are closely positioned to each other. This trajectory of cells can be linear, but can also bifurcate or follow more complex graph structures. The trajectory, therefore, enables the inference of gene expression dynamics and the ordering of cells by their progression through differentiation or response to external stimuli.
The method relies on the assumptions that the cells follow the same path through the process of interest and that their transcriptional state correlates to their progression. The algorithm can be applied to both mixed populations and temporal samples.

More than 50 methods for pseudo-temporal ordering have been developed, and each has its own requirements for prior information (such as starting cells or time course data), detectable topologies, and methodology.[[64]](#cite_note-64) An example algorithm is the Monocle algorithm[[65]](#cite_note-65) that carries out dimensionality reduction of the data, builds a [minimal spanning tree](/wiki/Minimal_spanning_tree "Minimal spanning tree") using the transformed data, orders cells in pseudo-time by following the longest connected path of the tree and consequently labels cells by type. Another example is the diffusion pseudotime (DPT) algorithm,[[63]](#cite_note-hag1-63) which uses a diffusion map and diffusion process. Another class of methods such as MARGARET [[66]](#cite_note-auto-66) employ graph partitioning for capturing complex trajectory topologies such as disconnected and multifurcating trajectories.

### Network inference

Gene regulatory network inference is a technique that aims to construct a network, shown as a graph, in which the [nodes](/wiki/Vertex_(graph_theory) "Vertex (graph theory)") represent the genes and edges indicate co-regulatory interactions. The method relies on the assumption that a strong statistical relationship between the expression of genes is an indication of a potential functional relationship.[[67]](#cite_note-67) The most commonly used method to measure the strength of a statistical relationship is [correlation](/wiki/Correlation "Correlation"). However, correlation fails to identify [non-linear](/wiki/Non-linear "Non-linear") relationships and [mutual information](/wiki/Mutual_information "Mutual information") is used as an alternative. Gene clusters linked in a network signify genes that undergo coordinated changes in expression.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

### Integration

The presence or strength of technical effects and the types of cells observed often differ in single-cell transcriptomics datasets generated using different experimental protocols and under different conditions. This difference results in strong [batch effects](/wiki/Batch_effects "Batch effects") that may bias the findings of statistical methods applied across batches, particularly in the presence of [confounding](/wiki/Confounding "Confounding").[[69]](#cite_note-69)
As a result of the aforementioned properties of single-cell transcriptomic data, batch correction methods developed for bulk sequencing data were observed to perform poorly. Consequently, researchers developed statistical methods to correct for batch effects that are robust to the properties of single-cell transcriptomic data to integrate data from different sources or experimental batches. Laleh Haghverdi performed foundational work in formulating the use of mutual nearest neighbors between each batch to define batch correction vectors.[[70]](#cite_note-70) With these vectors, you can merge datasets that each include at least one shared cell type. An orthogonal approach involves the projection of each dataset onto a shared low-dimensional space using [canonical correlation analysis](/wiki/Canonical_correlation_analysis "Canonical correlation analysis").[[71]](#cite_note-71) Mutual nearest neighbors and canonical correlation analysis have also been combined to define integration "anchors" comprising reference cells in one dataset, to which query cells in another dataset are normalized.[[72]](#cite_note-72) Another class of methods (e.g., scDREAMER[[73]](#cite_note-73)) uses deep generative models such as variational autoencoders for learning batch-invariant latent cellular representations which can be used for downstream tasks such as cell type clustering, denoising of single-cell gene expression vectors and trajectory inference.[[66]](#cite_note-auto-66)

# Single-Cell Transcriptomics: Current Methods and Challenges in Data Acquisition and Analysis

![
Asif Adil&#x;](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/1050896/74)
![Vijay Kumar&#x;](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/233204/74)
![Arif Tasleem Jan*](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/319453/74)
![Mohammed Asger*](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/1066039/74)

Rapid cost drops and advancements in next-generation sequencing have made profiling of cells at individual level a conventional practice in scientific laboratories worldwide. Single-cell transcriptomics [single-cell RNA sequencing (SC-RNA-seq)] has an immense potential of uncovering the novel basis of human life. The well-known heterogeneity of cells at the individual level can be better studied by single-cell transcriptomics. Proper downstream analysis of this data will provide new insights into the scientific communities. However, due to low starting materials, the SC-RNA-seq data face various computational challenges: normalization, differential gene expression analysis, dimensionality reduction, etc. Additionally, new methods like 10× Chromium can profile millions of cells in parallel, which creates a considerable amount of data. Thus, single-cell data handling is another big challenge. This paper reviews the single-cell sequencing methods, library preparation, and data generation. We highlight some of the main computational challenges that require to be addressed by introducing new bioinformatics algorithms and tools for analysis. We also show single-cell transcriptomics data as a big data problem.

## Introduction

The human body exhibits a diverse range of cells that undergo transit from one state to another in life (development, disease, and regeneration). Though derived from the same zygote, the cell, with its types and states, is greatly influenced by the internal processes and external factors ([Song et al., 2019](#B126)). In its progression through proliferation and the differentiation states to generate multiple cell types for organ formation, complex heterogeneities in the cellular architecture are observed. The cellular heterogeneity in terms of morphology, function, and gene expression profiles lie between various tissues, but has also been observed among the same cell types that allow them to perform different roles. Dysregulation in any particular cell type (irrespective of tissues, organs, and organ-system) influences the entire system that progresses to disorders and even severe diseases like cancer ([Macaulay et al., 2017](#B83)).

Recent technological advancements have enabled biologists to profile *cells at individual levels* on a variety of omics layers (genomes, transcriptomes, epigenomes, and proteomes) ([Hu et al., 2016](#B52)); among these, single cell (SC) transcriptomics is widely studied. The cells of a human body, being heterogeneous, often show a drastic variation at the individual level ([Wang and Bodovitz, 2010](#B147); [Xin et al., 2016](#B156)). The SC experiments were found much conclusive compared with bulk cell sequencing that involves sequencing in bulk (assuming cells of a particular type are identical) and estimating an average of expressions. The SC transcriptomics was awarded as method of the year by *Nature* in 2013 ([Xue et al., 2015](#B157)). With the advent of next-generation sequencing, it becomes possible to develop sequencing methods to probe the dynamics of the genome and variations thereof. Of them, RNA sequencing (RNA-seq)-mediated transcriptomic profiling revealed information of novel RNA species that deepened our understanding of the transcriptome dynamics ([Tang et al., 2009](#B132); [Wang et al., 2009](#B150); [Ozsolak and Milos, 2011](#B97)). Lately, these sequencing approaches have been extended to study intra-population heterogeneity of SCs ([Wills et al., 2013](#B153)), whereby it enabled the study of cell fates, their transition to different subtypes, and the dynamics of gene expression masked in bulk population studies ([Altschuler and Wu, 2010](#B3); [Trapnell et al., 2014](#B137)). Compared with bulk sequencing, where libraries are prepared from thousands of cells, libraries for single-cell RNA sequencing (SC-RNA-seq) are cell-specific towards investigating cellular functionalities of DNA and RNA in different cellular subsets ([Gross et al., 2015](#B44); [Xue et al., 2015](#B157)). Though SC-RNA-seq has revealed novel findings in different cellular backgrounds, it poses specific challenges: Pre-processing of the SC-RNA-seq data is majorly different from bulk RNA-seq, stricter protocols for library preparation and low starting material. Another challenge is the lack of analytical approaches required to accommodate large datasets generated during SC-RNA-seq experiments. Keeping this in view, we investigated the methods adopted in SC experiments, sequencing approaches, and challenges thereof, as part of realizing the goal of precision medicine.

## Single-Cell RNA Sequence Profiling Techniques

With the first report in 2009, a surge in the SC transcriptomics methods capable of sequencing millions of cells with great accuracy and viability in a short span of time was observed ([Tang et al., 2009](#B132)). These methods are generally different from each other in terms of cell isolation methods, cell lysis procedure, amplification process, cDNA generation, transcript coverage, and Unique Molecular Identifier (UMI) tagging (at either 3′ end or 5′ end). The most critical distinction in the SC-RNA profiling techniques is that some provide full-length transcript coverage and some only partially sequence from either 3′ end or 5′ end of the transcript ([Chen et al., 2019](#B23)). [Table 1](#T1) highlights widely used SC-RNA profiling methods in terms of different properties.

![www.frontiersin.org](https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/591122/fnins-15-591122-HTML/image_m/fnins-15-591122-t001.jpg)

**Table 1.** Current SC-RNA-seq profiling techniques, based on transcript coverage and UMI insertion possibility.

## Optimal Methodology of Single-Cell Transcriptomics

Of the various sequencing platforms, Drop-seq, InDrop, and 10× Chromium are well-known platforms for sequencing hundreds and thousands of cells in an unbiased manner ([Kulkarni et al., 2019](#B71)). In SC transcriptomics, each cell needs to be isolated from its originating tissue. The Droplet-based techniques, which at the core use microfluidics to attach cells with beads containing a unique barcode, are widely incorporated to separate cells. The performance criteria for isolation methods are based on three parameters: throughput, purity, and recovery ([Tomlinson et al., 2013](#B136); [Gross et al., 2015](#B44)). *Throughput* indicates the number of cells that can be isolated per unit time, *purity* refers to the number of cells collected after separation from tissue, and *recovery* is the final amount of the target cells, in hand, after separation. The morphological complexity of cells like those of the central nervous system (CNS) makes the separation process a little challenging. The segregation process exposes them to specific environmental, chemical, and harsh dissociation steps that often bias data analysis ([Kulkarni et al., 2019](#B71)). The dissociation of intact cells from a frozen postmortem tissue is also challenging, as cell membranes are prone to damage from mechanical and physical stresses as part of the freeze–thaw process ([McGann et al., 1988](#B87)). Though each cell separation methods currently in use shows an advantage different for the above three parameters, it becomes imperative to select a well-suited method for the isolation of a cell. The current methodology of cell separation is broadly categorized into two groups based on (1) cellular properties like cell density, cell shape, cell size, etc., and (2) biological characteristics of a cell that comprises affinity methods ([Tomlinson et al., 2013](#B136)). [Tables 2](#T2), [3](#T3) show some of the widely used methods concerning the operational mode, throughput, advantages, and disadvantages.

**Table 2.** Commonly used methods for cell isolation based on biological characteristics.

**Table 3.** Commonly used methods for cell isolation on the bases of physical characteristics.

Though high-throughput SC-RNA approaches such as 10× Chromium allows analysis of cells in an unbiased manner, it lacks in providing an in-depth information on sequence diversity, splicing, and chimeric transcripts generated in the process ([Lebrigand et al., 2020](#B76)). The problem is overcome by performing Nanopore long-read sequencing [using a cell barcode (cellBC) assignment to long reads] to obtain a full-length sequence corresponding to the 10× Chromium system’s data. As SC library preparation requires robust amplification, chimeric cDNA generation and amplification bias issues are currently addressed by employing a 3′ or 5′ end tag-based approach ([Trombetta et al., 2015](#B139); [Natarajan et al., 2019](#B93)). The sequence length method determines the quality of alignment across the total length of a gene, while tag-based methods integrate UMIs at either 3′ end or 5′ end of the transcript ([Kivioja et al., 2012](#B66); [Smith et al., 2017](#B125); [Sena et al., 2018](#B116)). The UMI addition makes it easier to identify and quantify the individual transcripts by eliminating PCR artifacts and minimizes false annotation of PCR-generated chimeric cDNAs as novel transcripts. The full length-based methodology provides an all-inclusive coverage of the reads, yet they contribute a bias for long genes, as the genes with shorter length are often missed ([Phipson et al., 2017](#B102)). Additionally, the higher sequencing error rate of long-read sequencers and UMI problems account for a serious issue pertaining to these platforms ([Gupta et al., 2018](#B46); [Lebrigand et al., 2020](#B76); [Volden and Vollmers, 2020](#B145)). Despite this, the Tag-based methods have shown a fair dominance in SC-RNA library preparation for quantifying the transcripts in SC analysis when cell number is large ([Figure 1](#F1)).

**Figure 1.** Single-cell analysis in disease and health. Starting from the dissociation of target cells from the target tissue/organ, their isolation based on fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS) or other microfluidic techniques to RNA extraction. The RNA extraction is followed by cDNA synthesis by reverse transcriptase, followed by amplification and sequencing. From the sequencing, the reads are aligned and subjected to quantification that results in a quantification matrix or Gene Expression Matrix.

## Quantification of Expression and Quality Control

Like bulk RNA-seq, the transcripts in SC-RNA are sequenced into reads that generate the raw fastq data. The quality of the sequence reads generated in a sequencing method is considered an important quality indicator of SC-RNA-seq data. As the alignment of the transcript reads for SC-RNA-seq is same as bulk RNA-seq, the methods and tools used for the gene or transcript quantification for bulk RNA-seq can also be used for quantifying transcripts generated by SC-RNA-seq ([Li and Homer, 2010](#B80); [Fonseca et al., 2012](#B40)). HISAT2 ([Kim et al., 2019](#B62)), TopHat2 ([Kim et al., 2013](#B63)), and STAR ([Dobin et al., 2013](#B32)) are currently the most popular alignment tools, which can map billions of reads to a reference transcriptome with greater accuracy and high speed. Transcriptome reconstruction can be either *de novo* (for samples lacking reference genome) or reference based, also called genome-guided assembly ([Chen et al., 2011](#B24)). However, the former technique sometimes lacks accuracy in comparison with the reference-based assembly approach ([Garber et al., 2011](#B42)). For SC-RNA-seq methods that generate data on a whole-transcriptome basis, Smart-seq2 ([Picelli et al., 2014](#B103)) and MATQ-seq ([Sheng and Zong, 2019](#B120)) use Cufflinks, RSEM, Stringtie, etc., for the quantification of transcripts, while methods that incorporate the 3′ end UMI tagging [like Drop-seq ([Macosko et al., 2015](#B84)), InDrop ([Klein et al., 2015](#B67)), MARS-seq ([Jaitin et al., 2014](#B58)), etc.] require specific algorithms to generate the expression count for the transcript. Another efficient tool for the UMI-based methods was developed by [Huang and Sanguinetti (2017)](#B53) for calculating the expression count of SCs accurately. [Table 4](#T4) provides information about the current tools for read alignment and expression quantification. The SC-RNA-seq exhibits certain limitations, which results in higher technical noise ([Kolodziejczyk et al., 2015](#B69)). In SC-RNA-seq data, many transcripts appear to be lost during reverse transcription due to the small number and low capture efficiency of RNA molecules in SCs ([Saliba et al., 2014](#B113)). Consequently, in one cell, some transcripts are highly expressed but are missing in another cell. This pattern is described as a “dropout” event. It has been reported that even the most sensitive protocol for SC-RNA-seq fails to detect some of the transcripts as part of Dropout events ([Haque et al., 2017](#B48)). When the cells are dissociated or isolated, a certain number of cells become dead or get destroyed. The SC-RNA-seq methods generate low-quality data from these cells ([Ilicic et al., 2016](#B55)). After alignment and quantification of the transcripts, the quality control check of cells is necessary to remove low-quality cells for an accurate downstream analysis.

## Challenges Impeding Single-Cell RNA sequence Data Analysis

Though SC-RNA-seq has deepened our understanding of the cellular heterogeneity and molecular basis of life, it is impeded by several technical and computational challenges. The foremost among them is that its datasets exhibit a considerable amount of noise attributed to meager starting materials that often causes faulty downstream analysis and erroneous results ([Brennecke et al., 2013](#B16)). The SC-RNA-seq data analysis is performed as subtle execution in computational steps; read alignment, expression count generation, cell quality control, normalizing the data, and then further downstream analysis including SC clustering, differential gene expression (DGE), pseudo-temporal analysis, etc. In addition to low starting materials, the technical noise in the datasets is contributed by various factors, like batch effects ([Haghverdi et al., 2018](#B47)) and the low capture efficiency of protocols ([Hwang et al., 2018](#B54)). A few of the analytical steps, including read alignment and generation of count matrix, can be resolved using already available computational methods designed for bulk RNA-seq. However, data processing tasks like normalization, DGE analysis, cell imputation, and dimensionality reduction, etc., call for the development of novel computational techniques, algorithms, and tools for smooth execution of SC-RNA-seq data analysis. The nature of the challenges that SC-RNA-seq data possess, including big data problem ([Costa, 2012](#B28); [Yu and Lin, 2016](#B159); [Angerer et al., 2017](#B5); [He et al., 2017](#B50)), is highlighted in the following subsections:

## Normalization

In SC-RNA-seq, coverage of sequences between the libraries exhibit systematic differences from experimental procedures, dropout events, depth of the sequencing, and other technical effects ([Stegle et al., 2015](#B127)). These differences must be corrected by normalizing the data such that there is no interference in the comparison of the gene expression between cells. Being crucial, normalization of the SC-RNA-seq datasets eventually leads to lucid downstream analysis, including identifying different cell subsets and revealing differential expression of genes. In bulk RNA-seq, expression counts from various libraries are usually normalized by computing the fragments per kilobase of transcript counts of per million mapped fragments (FPKM) ([Mortazavi et al., 2008](#B92)), transcripts per million (TPM) ([Li and Dewey, 2011](#B79)), reads per kilobase of transcripts per million mapped reads (RPKM), upper quartile (UQ) ([Bullard et al., 2010](#B18)), DESeq ([Love et al., 2014](#B81)), removed unwanted variation (RUV) ([Risso et al., 2014](#B109)), and Gamma regression model ([Ding et al., 2015](#B31)). Generally, there are two types of normalization: (1) normalization of data within the sample, and (2) normalization of the data between the sample ([Vallejos et al., 2015](#B140), [2017](#B141)). In the former, FPKM/RPKM or TPM are used to exclude gene-specific biases ([Vallejos et al., 2017](#B141)) such as guanine–cytosine (GC) content and gene length, while in the latter, the normalization method tunes the sample-specific differences such as sequencing depth and capture efficiency. While ignoring the underlying stochasticity, normalization generates a relative expression estimate ([Stegle et al., 2015](#B127)), assuming the overall processed RNA per sample is equal ([AlJanahi et al., 2018](#B2); [Olsen and Baryawno, 2018](#B96)). The bulk-based strategies for normalization have been reported unsuitable for SC-RNA-seq datasets because the datasets are highly zero-inflated and have higher technical noise. Multiple methods have been developed for normalizing the SC-RNA-seq data ([Vallejos et al., 2015](#B140); [Lun et al., 2016](#B82); [Sengupta et al., 2016](#B117); [Bacher et al., 2017](#B9); [Yip et al., 2017](#B158)). However, *O*(*nlogn*) is considered more efficient than others in performing normalization of SC-RNA-seq data ([Yip et al., 2017](#B158)).

### Dimensionality Reduction

High dimensionality is yet another challenge that SC-RNA-seq data present. Owing to the data coming from cells showing high dimensions, i.e., a large number of genes, it is necessary to reduce (while optimally preserving the critical properties) the set of random variables and work with the principle variables which describe the data profoundly ([Andrews and Hemberg, 2019](#B4)). The two most frequently used methods for dimensionality reduction are principal component analysis (PCA) ([Van Der Maaten et al., 2009](#B143)) and T-distribution stochastic neighbor embedding (t-SNE) ([Van Der Maaten and Hinton, 2008](#B142); [Kobak and Berens, 2019](#B68)). PCA uses a linear process to transform a set of variables (possibly correlated) into an uncorrelated variable known as a principal component, while t-SNE is a non-linear probability distribution-based approach. Both PCA and t-SNE methods of dimensionality reduction have certain limitations ([Chen et al., 2019](#B23)); based on the assumption that approximately all the data are distributed normally, PCA does not effectively amount to the underlying complexities in the structure of SC-RNA-seq data, and t-SNE has a larger time complexity reaching *O*(*n*2) ([Pezzotti et al., 2017](#B101)). The most recent algorithm employed for dimensionality reduction “UMAP” (Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection) ([McInnes et al., 2018](#B88); [Becht et al., 2019](#B12)) outperforms PCA and t-SNE for SC-RNA-seq in terms of high reproducibility and meaningful organization of cells ([Becht et al., 2018](#B11)). UMAP is a non-linear graph-based algorithm that tends to identify the closest neighbors of a data point and assigns them a larger weight, thereby preserving the topological structure of the data. The idea is to project a low-dimensional representation of the data while preserving the nearest neighbours of an individual data point (i.e., cells). This helps to group more closely related neighbours and partly conserves the relation of points in the “long-range” using the intermediate data points. Although the interpretation of the distances in a reduced space becomes difficult, UMAP has been largely able to uncover the elusive features of the data. UMAP is computationally faster than t-SNE, preserves the global structure, and maintains the continuity of cell subsets ([Becht et al., 2018](#B11)). At the core, UMAP assumes the subsistence of a “manifold structure” in the data. This assumption makes it find the manifolds in the noise of data. Since SC-RNA-seq suffers from a significant amount of noise, it is necessary to consider it before applying UMAP ([McInnes et al., 2018](#B88)).

Another method to perform dimensionality reduction is the linear discriminant analysis (LDA). LDA is a supervised dimensionality reduction method that tends to maximize the separability between the predetermined classes, using the covariance of “between-class” and “within-class.” It first calculates the mean of the distances between the classes and then the mean of distances within the classes. The goal is to find a projection to maximize the ratio of between-class variability to the lower within-class variability ([Tharwat et al., 2017](#B134); [Qiao and Meister, 2020](#B104)).

The SC-RNA-seq exhibits potential challenges similar to text mining, such as polysemy and synonymy, noise, and sparsity. Recently, a popular text mining technique, latent semantic analysis (LSA), has been used in SC-RNA-seq dimensionality reduction ([Cheng et al., 2019](#B26)). LSA at core uses a linear algebra-based method, called singular value decomposition (SVD), to cluster the semantically similar terms. SVD approximates a low-rank matrix to the given cell-gene matrix, such that the dimensions of the new matrix are much less than the original. This approximation is made by taking a combined product of the matrices of left-singular vector, right-singular vector, and the diagonal singular values.

### Differential Gene Expression Analysis

The expression of genes is stochastic in a cell; expression values thus observed are quite heterogeneous at the individual level among seemingly similar cells. The DGE analysis helps to understand the innate cellular processes and stochasticity of gene expressions ([McDavid et al., 2013](#B86)). The problem faced in DGE analysis is identifying genes that are largely expressed in a group of cells without any or no preliminary information of primary cell subtypes ([Stegle et al., 2015](#B127)). Additionally, gene expressions in individual cells show multimodality ([Kippner et al., 2014](#B64)). As expression variability of genes between cells of the same type indicates transcriptional heterogeneity ([Johnson et al., 2015](#B59); [Angermueller et al., 2016](#B6)), it needs robust computational approaches to detect the true heterogeneity. In addition to multimodality, the sparsity due to—but not limited to—dropout events brings irregularities in the data, consequent of which the differential genes are difficult to detect. Various parametric as well as non-parametric approaches like Single-cell Differential Expression, Model-based Analysis of Single-cell Transcriptome (MAST), D3E, scDD, SigEMD, and DEsingle ([Kharchenko et al., 2014](#B60); [Finak et al., 2015](#B39); [Delmans and Hemberg, 2016](#B30); [Korthauer et al., 2016](#B70); [Miao et al., 2018](#B91); [Wang and Nabavi, 2018](#B149)) have been developed/proposed for the DGE analysis in the SC-RNA-seq data. However, these tools try to manage either the gene dropouts or multimodality ([Wang et al., 2019](#B148)). For the subtle DGE analysis, these two crucial challenges need to be taken care of together.

### Cluster Analysis

Cluster analysis of SC-RNA-seq data is required to identify both known and unknown rare cell types ([Menon, 2018](#B90)). Along with the technical dropout events, the cells show a huge variation in gene expression levels even from the same set. As mentioned above, SC-RNA-seq suffers from massive inflation of zeros. There are three reasons for the observation of zeros in data: (1) the transcript was absent explicitly, hence a “true zero”; (2) the depth of sequencing was very low, and the transcript was present but not accounted for; and (3) at the time of library preparation, the transcript could not be captured or failed to amplify. The measurements from the latter two are considered to be the “false zeros.” The concentration of too many zeros in the data brings in irregularities. These technical and biological factors lead to significant noise, due to which cluster analysis becomes challenging. For this, methods like Seurat, DropClust, and SCANPY ([Satija et al., 2015](#B114); [Ntranos et al., 2016](#B94); [Yip et al., 2017](#B158); [Sinha et al., 2018](#B122)) have been proposed for clustering of SCs. There are certain limitations associated with these as well. Seurat and SCANPY work well with large datasets but underperforms when the dataset is smaller ([Kiselev et al., 2019](#B65)). The anticipated complexity in data and the rate of generation of SC data will be a challenge for all these tools. UMAP is yet another method for cluster identification of SC-RNA-seq data; however, as UMAP tends to preserve the local-topological structure, it is rather difficult to establish a relationship between clusters when the underlying cell subtypes are unknown.

In addition to the sparsity in data, SC-RNA-seq data suffer from a huge level of noise from faulty experimental designs usually referred to as “batch-effects.” The noise in the data may contribute to the overfitting of the data. The overfitting can be avoided using regularization. Regularization is a process of restricting or reducing the features at the time of modeling.

So far, the clustering methods cluster the cells as per the transcription similarity, but the biological annotation of cell clusters remains a challenge. A possible solution could come from the generation of the data itself, as the more data are accumulated, the more can unknown clusters be matched with the previously known clusters. Another popular approach for cluster annotation is to use Gene Ontology (GO) analysis of the marker genes ([Ashburner et al., 2000](#B8)).

### Single-Cell Spatial Transcriptomics and RNA Velocity

Spatial transcriptomics (ST) gives measurement of gene expression changes with reference to geographical coordinates of the cells in tissues. It allows measurements of the transcripts with an advantage of conserving the spatial information, providing an additional analytical edge ([Burgess, 2019](#B19)). ST conform to *in situ* methods like seqFISH ([Shah et al., 2016](#B119)), seqFISH+ ([Eng et al., 2019](#B36)), FISSEQ (Fluorescence *in situ* Sequence) ([Lee et al., 2015](#B78)), MERFISH ([Chen et al., 2015](#B25)), and SC-RNA-seq-based methods like slide-seq ([Rodriques et al., 2019](#B110)) and Niche-seq ([Medaglia et al., 2017](#B89)). *In situ* labeling of the transcripts in tissues is advantageous for visualizing the location; however, a chance of molecular overcrowding results in fluorescence signal overlap. This overcrowding can be overcome by using SC spatial RNA-seq; however, the dissociation of cells prior to sequencing makes it difficult to link the transcriptomes back to their original locations ([Burgess, 2019](#B19)). These complementary strengths and limitations make it necessary to integrate the datasets generated by each technology.

In ST, a pair of images are generated, one containing whole tissue with fairly visible spots and the other having clearly visible fluorescence array spots ([Wong et al., 2018](#B154)). To leverage the ST, the image data from ST need to be integrated with the SC-RNA-seq data. As the principle challenges in both ST and SC-RNA-seq are the sparsity of the data and noise from technical and biological sources, an accurate data normalization and transformation is necessary before any downstream analysis ([Wagner et al., 2016](#B146)). Few tools have been developed to determine the cell types with respect to their spatial identities ([Edsgärd et al., 2018](#B34); [Svensson et al., 2018](#B131); [Dries et al., 2019](#B33); [Queen et al., 2019](#B105)). These tools lack interactive processing of images and fails in providing a comprehensive three-dimensional view of the tissue. Recently, STUtility ([Bergenstråhle et al., 2020b](#B14))—an R package using non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) for reducing the dimensions, spatial correlation (based on Pearson correlation), and K-means clustering—was found capable of providing a holistic view of the expression in tissues. SpatialCPie ([Bergenstråhle et al., 2020a](#B13)) is another easy-to-use R package that uses clustering at various resolutions to interactively uncover the gene expression patterns. [Elosua-Bayes et al. (2021)](#B35) developed SPOTlight, which uses NMF along with non-negative least squares (NNLS). NMF helps in dimensional reduction, followed by selection of marker genes using seurat package and then using NNLS to deconvolute each captured location ([Elosua-Bayes et al., 2021](#B35)).

Highlights
----------

*   •This study presents a practical guide to scRNA-seq analysis in neuroscience. 
*   •We provide a step-by-step Seurat tutorial for comparing gene expression between conditions. 
*   •The workflow highlights QC, integration, clustering, and biological interpretation steps. 
*   •We use Docker to offer an automated and reproducible single-cell analysis environment. 

Abstract
--------

Single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) has revolutionized our ability to analyze gene expression at the single-cell level, providing unprecedented insights into cellular heterogeneity, rare cell populations, and dynamic cellular processes. In neuroscience, scRNA-seq has enabled the identification of diverse brain cell types, elucidation of developmental pathways, and discovery of mechanisms underlying neurological diseases. This tutorial provides a practical guide to scRNA-seq data analysis in neuroscience, focusing on the essential workflows and theoretical foundations. Key steps covered include quality control, data preprocessing, integration, cell clustering, and differential expression analysis. Using the Seurat R package, the tutorial demonstrates a comparative analysis approach for identifying differentially expressed genes between conditions, emphasizing the biological interpretation of results. By addressing the unique challenges of scRNA-seq data and illustrating methods for robust analysis, this work aims to enhance the reliability and reproducibility of scRNA-seq studies in neuroscience, supporting the exploration of cellular mechanisms and advancing research into brain function and disease.

1. Introduction
---------------

Single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) is a powerful technology for deciphering the gene expression profiles of individual cells ([Piwecka et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010225000653#bib19)). Unlike conventional bulk RNA sequencing, which captures the average gene expression profiles of cell populations, scRNA-seq provides detailed biological insights by enabling the analysis of cellular heterogeneity, identifying rare cell types, and investigating the dynamics of cells during development or pathological conditions.

In neuroscience, scRNA-seq has greatly advanced our understanding of cell populations and the regulatory mechanisms driving biological phenomena. scRNA-seq has revealed the diverse composition of cell populations in the brain and nervous tissues, uncovering previously unknown cell types and subtypes ([Yao et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010225000653#bib30)). Notably, applying scRNA-seq to developmental studies has elucidated the detailed dynamics of cellular differentiation and maturation during brain development and neural circuit formation ([Eze et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010225000653#bib7); [Alamin et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010225000653#bib2); [Zeng et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010225000653#bib31)). In the context of disease research, scRNA-seq has deepened our understanding of pathological mechanisms and enabled the identification of biomarkers for neurological disorders, including Alzheimer’s disease ([Wang et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010225000653#bib26)), Parkinson’s disease ([Kamath et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010225000653#bib12)), and autism spectrum disorder ([Velmeshev et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010225000653#bib23); [Wamsley et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010225000653#bib25)).

The emergence of specialized toolkits for scRNA-seq analysis, such as Seurat and Scanpy, has made the analysis process more accessible than ever. Moreover, resources like official websites, documentation, and tutorial blogs offer invaluable support for researchers performing scRNA-seq analysis. However, relying on specialized software can lead to a "black-box" effect, obscuring the details of analytical processes and their theoretical foundations. A solid understanding of these processes and principles enables users to troubleshoot errors, interpret unexpected results, and ensure the validity of their analyses.

This review offers a practical tutorial on scRNA-seq data analysis in neuroscience, with detailed explanations of analytical steps and their theoretical foundations. Specifically, it focuses on comparative analysis of datasets from different conditions, emphasizing the interpretation of biological differences. Unlike bulk RNA-seq, scRNA-seq analysis introduces unique challenges and methodologies, broadly divided into three stages: quality control (QC) and preprocessing, primary analysis (e.g., cell clustering, cell type annotation, and differential expression analysis), and secondary analysis (e.g., lineage inference, regulon prediction, and cell-cell communication analysis, tailored to specific research goals). This review covers methods up to differential expression analysis, a key aspect of comparative analysis, while excluding secondary analysis from its scope. To illustrate these methods, we provide a step-by-step guide to comparing scRNA-seq datasets from two conditions, identifying differentially expressed genes in specific cell types using the Seurat package in R.

2. Comparative analysis of scRNA-seq data
-----------------------------------------

Gaining biological insights requires identifying key genes and pathways relevant to the biological phenomena and systems under study. Differentially expressed genes and pathways between conditions likely reflect the causes or consequences of these differences, enhancing our understanding of biological mechanisms and guiding new research directions. For instance, comparing cell populations and gene expression between healthy and diseased tissues can reveal disease progression and underlying pathological mechanisms. Similarly, analyzing gene expression before and after treatment aids in evaluating therapeutic efficacy and uncovering mechanisms of action.

3. Data analysis procedures
---------------------------

### 3.1. Characteristics of scRNA-seq data

In scRNA-seq experiments, dissociated cells or nuclei are first processed to prepare a DNA library suitable for high-throughput sequencing. This step, known as library preparation, is followed by sequencing the DNA library using a high-throughput DNA sequencer to generate nucleic sequence data (read data). The read data is then mapped to the genome using tools like Cell Ranger, producing a read count matrix (gene expression matrix) that summarizes the number of mapped reads for each gene. This matrix serves as the foundation for scRNA-seq data analysis.

In the gene expression matrix, rows represent genes, columns correspond to cells, and each element indicates the read count (or the number of Unique Molecular Identifiers (UMIs), if UMIs are used). This count reflects the number of mRNA copies derived from each gene in each cell. For example, if a specific gene is highly expressed in a cell, the corresponding element in the matrix (row for the gene, column for the cell) will have a high value. Each row captures the expression level of a gene across all cells, while each column represents the gene expression profile of a single cell ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010225000653#fig0005)).

### 3.2. Quality control (QC) of data

#### 3.2.1. Data QC and QC parameters

The first step in scRNA-seq analysis is quality control (QC), which ensures the success of the experiment across the dataset and filters out low-quality cells. Low-quality cells include those with low viability (e.g., dead or damaged cells, or cells with degraded RNA) and doublets or multiplets (two or more than two cells mistakenly counted as one). These cells can introduce noise into downstream analyses and should be removed early in the process.

Several QC parameters are commonly used for scRNA-seq data evaluation. One important metric is the total count (UMI count) and the total number of detected genes. Extremely low values for these metrics may indicate cells with low viability, whereas extremely high values can suggest the presence of doublets. Another key parameter is the percentage of mitochondrial gene expression (%mt) relative to the total gene expression. When the cell membrane is damaged, free RNA degrades faster than mitochondrial RNA, leading to higher %mt levels. Thus, cells with elevated %mt are considered damaged and are typically excluded from analysis. In addition, the percentage of ribosomal RNA gene expression (%rRNA) serves as an indicator of cell state. A high %rRNA level reflects a greater proportion of cytoplasmic mRNA, while a low %rRNA indicates a higher proportion of nuclear mRNA.

#### 3.2.2. Automated QC tools

Traditionally, QC parameters were visualized using violin plots or similar methods, and abnormal cells were manually removed based on visual inspection or fixed thresholds. However, to minimize subjectivity, automated approaches to QC have been developed.

**ddqc** is a tool that automates QC by determining thresholds for QC parameters in a data-driven manner and excluding low-quality cells accordingly ([Subramanian et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010225000653#bib22)). The tool operates on the hypothesis that optimal QC thresholds may vary depending on cell states. Specifically, it performs unsupervised clustering of cells, calculates the median absolute deviation (MAD) for each QC parameter within each cluster, and determines cluster-specific thresholds (default: 2 ×MAD). Cells falling outside these thresholds are classified as low-quality and excluded.

**DoubletFinder** is a specialized tool for detecting and removing doublets ([McGinnis et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010225000653#bib17)). It identifies doublets based on the assumption that cells resembling artificially generated doublets are likely to be true doublets. Artificial doublets are simulated by averaging the expression profiles of randomly paired cells within the dataset. Cells that closely match these artificial doublets are flagged as doublets. Benchmark studies have demonstrated that DoubletFinder outperforms other doublet detection tools ([Xi and Li, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010225000653#bib29)).

There are two key advantages to using automated QC tools. First, they enable more efficient and accurate QC compared to manual methods, streamlining data processing. Second, they enhance reproducibility by providing consistent QC results based on data-driven criteria, eliminating the subjectivity of manual inspection and arbitrary threshold settings. Automated QC tools are poised to significantly improve the reliability of scRNA-seq data analysis.

### 3.3. Data preprocessing

Data preprocessing is a critical step in scRNA-seq analysis to enhance the accuracy and reliability of downstream analyses. This section covers key preprocessing steps, including normalization, detection of highly variable genes (HVGs), standardization, and principal component analysis (PCA).

#### 3.3.1. Normalization

Since the total number of sequenced reads varies between cells, raw gene read counts are not directly comparable. Normalization converts these raw counts into values that are comparable across cells and samples.

In Seurat, the NormalizeData function performs normalization by dividing each gene’s expression value by the total number of reads for the respective cell (nCounts), scaling the data so that the total detected reads per cell equal 10,000, and applying a log transformation. This process stabilizes variance independently of the mean, enhancing analysis accuracy. Gene expression levels typically follow a heavy-tailed distribution, where most genes cluster around zero while a few exhibit large expression values. This imbalance can cause highly expressed genes to dominate the analysis, an issue mitigated by log transformation.

#### 3.3.2. Detection of highly variable genes (HVGs)

Genes with high variability in expression across cells often reflect underlying biological differences and are referred to as highly variable genes (HVGs). These genes are crucial for distinguishing between different cell states or types. For example, SLC6A1, which is the marker gene specific to GABAergic neurons, is highly expressed in these neurons but exhibits low expression in other cell types. In a mixed population of neuronal subtypes and non-neuronal cells, SLC6A1 is expected to show high expression variability across cells. In contrast, genes with low variability typically include housekeeping genes or genes with uniformly low expression. These genes contribute little to understanding biological differences, such as cell types or states, and are therefore often excluded from downstream analyses like clustering.

In Seurat, the FindVariableFeatures function is used to identify highly variable genes (HVGs), reducing noise and improving data reliability. RNA sequencing data typically contains genes with widely varying expression levels, which results in large differences in variance. In raw data, genes with higher mean expression often exhibit greater variance. The FindVariableFeatures function employs a default method called variance stabilizing transformation (VST) to correct for this dependency, ensuring that variance is independent of mean expression. This adjustment allows for direct comparison of gene variability across expression levels. By default, the top 2000 genes with the highest adjusted variance (standardized variance) are selected as HVGs.

Focusing on HVGs also offers significant computational benefits. scRNA-seq data is inherently high-dimensional, and analyzing all genes demands substantial computational resources. By restricting the analysis to HVGs, the dimensionality of the data is reduced, significantly lowering computation time and memory usage. This approach enhances the efficiency of the analysis while retaining the most informative features of the dataset.

#### 3.3.3. Standardization

Before conducting PCA, data standardization is essential. Without standardization, genes with higher expression levels can disproportionately influence PCA results, overshadowing genes with lower expression. Standardization transforms each gene’s data to have a mean of 0 and a standard deviation of 1 (z-score normalization), enabling fair comparison across genes. In Seurat, the ScaleData function performs this standardization, and by default, it is applied exclusively to highly variable genes (HVGs).

#### 3.3.4. Principal component analysis (PCA)

Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is a widely used dimensionality reduction technique applicable to scRNA-seq analysis and other types of high-dimensional data, where high dimensionality refers to a large number of genes. High-dimensional data often contains substantial noise, while meaningful information is typically concentrated in a lower-dimensional manifold—a concept known as the manifold hypothesis. PCA extracts the primary structure of high-dimensional data, compressing it into a lower-dimensional space while preserving essential information ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010225000653#fig0010)(a)). This process can also reduce noise as a byproduct. By lowering the dimensionality, PCA reduces the computational burden for downstream analyses. In Seurat, PCA is performed using the RunPCA function.

### 3.4. Data integration

Once QC and preprocessing are complete, the next step is data integration—a crucial process for aligning cell types and states across different datasets to enable meaningful comparisons under varying conditions. This step is essential for integrating datasets from different experimental batches, donors, or conditions, forming a core part of the scRNA-seq analysis workflow.

In Seurat V5, the IntegrateLayers function has been introduced for data integration. This function integrates multiple data objects stored within the same layer of a single Seurat object ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010225000653#fig0010)(a)). Among the various methods Seurat offers for data integration, canonical correlation analysis (CCA) is one of the most widely used.

CCA identifies common cell types across datasets by finding similarities in gene expression patterns. For instance, in neuronal cell datasets from different experiments, neurons of the same type exhibit similar gene expression profiles. CCA projects these datasets into a shared lower-dimensional space ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010225000653#fig0010)(b)), where cells with comparable expression patterns are placed closer together, enhancing consistency across datasets.

The core of CCA involves optimizing a projection matrix to maximize the correlation between the low-dimensional representations of different datasets. This transformation highlights common cell types, facilitating unified analysis in downstream clustering steps ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010225000653#fig0010)(b)). By reducing batch effects and variations due to experimental conditions, CCA enables more reliable cell classification.

In scRNA-seq analysis, particularly in neuroscience, accurately capturing the correspondence of cell types across datasets is vital. Data integration using CCA is a robust approach for achieving this, ensuring consistency and reliability in comparative analyses.

### 3.5. Cell clustering

Cell clustering involves grouping cells with similar gene expression profiles. Cells with similar expression patterns are likely to share common biological properties, making clustering an essential preprocessing step for cell type annotation and cross-condition comparisons.

In Seurat, clustering is efficiently performed using the FindNeighbors and FindClusters functions. The default clustering algorithm is the Louvain method [12], a widely used community detection approach. The process begins with the construction of a cell similarity network using the FindNeighbors function, which calculates k-nearest neighbors (kNN) and shared nearest neighbors (SNN) to build a similarity graph. Subsequently, the FindClusters function identifies communities within this network, grouping cells with similar expression patterns into distinct clusters.

To evaluate the accuracy of clustering, nonlinear dimensionality reduction techniques such as UMAP or t-SNE are commonly employed ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010225000653#fig0010)(a)). These methods reduce the data to two or three dimensions, making it easier to visualize. In Seurat, UMAP can be executed with the RunUMAP function, and clustering results can be visualized in two-dimensional space using the DimPlot function, enabling users to confirm the clustering outcomes visually.

### 3.6. Cell type annotation

One of the core tasks in scRNA-seq data analysis is cell type annotation, which involves assigning specific cell type labels to individual cells based on their gene expression profiles. For instance, in single-cell transcriptome data from brain tissue, cells might be labeled as "glutamatergic neuron," "GABAergic neuron," or "astrocyte" ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010225000653#fig0010)(b)).

Traditionally, cell type annotation is performed manually by examining the expression of known marker genes for each cell type. While effective, this method is time-intensive and prone to subjective bias. To overcome these limitations, various automated tools and methods have been developed. Two representative approaches are described below.

#### 3.6.1. Marker gene-based methods

Marker gene-based methods annotate cells by utilizing the expression information of known marker genes for each cell type. One prominent tool in this category is **ScType** ([Ianevski et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0168010225000653#bib10)), which automatically assigns cell type labels to clusters obtained through prior clustering, based on marker gene data.

To use ScType, the scRNA-seq data must first be pre-clustered. Additionally, a marker gene database is required, which includes cell markers for both humans and mice. This database accounts for both positive and negative markers, with the inclusion of negative markers significantly improving the accuracy of cell type annotation.

Single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) involves isolating and sequencing RNA from individual cells, enabling comprehensive analysis of gene expression across diverse cellular populations.

Dynamic transcriptome analysis assesses changes in gene expression over time, providing insights into cellular processes, differentiation pathways, and responses to external stimuli in individual cells.
